claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's start by determining if you have a qualifying child.

**How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me about each of them and their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! I'm inquiring about my two children. Alex is 13 and Blake is 15. They're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information! Since both Alex (13) and Blake (15) are under 16 years old, they automatically meet the age qualification. Let me continue with the next check for both of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about their work status.

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

This is typically the case for most children under 16, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work. They're both still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great, thank you for confirming. Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they both meet this requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

(This is usually the case for children who are in school and not working.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to check about apprenticeships.

**Are Alex and Blake in any apprenticeships?**

Since you mentioned they're both in school, I assume they are not in an apprenticeship, but I just want to confirm. 

(For reference: most apprenticeships disqualify a child from Child Benefit, except Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales which are approved.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in apprenticeships. They're just doing their regular schooling.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you for confirming. Since neither Alex nor Blake are in apprenticeships, let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You are a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you? For most people living in the UK, the first option (normally lives in the UK) will apply.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you. Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You qualify if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with the children** – They live in your home with you
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** – You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** – You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me. Blake doesn't live with me, but I do help out financially - I contribute towards their upkeep with things like clothes and other bits they need.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. It sounds like:
- **Alex** lives with you at home
- **Blake** doesn't live with you, but you contribute to their upkeep financially

Both of these count as being responsible for the child, so you meet this requirement for both children. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Important question now:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex and/or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Please let me know:
1. For **Alex** (who lives with you) – is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for them?
2. For **Blake** (who doesn't live with you) – is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for them?

If someone else is claiming, do you have an agreement about who should receive the benefit, or is there a dispute?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. As for Blake, no one else is claiming for them either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, we can proceed to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check if any special circumstances apply to where Alex and Blake are living.

**Are either Alex or Blake currently:**

1. **In hospital** for an extended period?
2. **In local authority care?**
3. **Being fostered?**
4. **Living normally** (at home, at school, with family, etc.)?

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex lives at home with you
- Blake lives elsewhere but you contribute to their upkeep

Would you say both children are in "normal living" situations (i.e., not in hospital, care, or a fostering arrangement)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally. Blake has been in local authority care for a bit, but not for very long.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important detail. Let me check the rules for each child separately.

For **Alex**, who lives at home normally, I can continue with the standard checks.

For **Blake**, who is in local authority care, I need to check the specific rules around care duration. Let me look into that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Here's what the rules say about **Blake** being in local authority care:

**For Blake**, Child Benefit can continue if **either** of these applies:
- Blake has been in care for **8 weeks or less**, OR
- Blake spends at least **24 hours a week** at home with you

You can also reclaim if Blake:
- Spends at least **2 consecutive nights per week** at home, OR
- Stays at home for **7 or more days in a row**

You mentioned Blake has been in care "not for very long." 

**Has Blake been in local authority care for 8 weeks or less? Or does Blake spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been about a month - so well under 8 weeks. Blake doesn't spend 24 hours a week at home though.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since Blake has been in care for about a month (around 4 weeks), which is well under the 8-week limit, you still qualify for Child Benefit for Blake. The 24 hours at home rule is an alternative option, so you only need to meet one of the conditions.

Let me continue with the next check for Blake, and then we'll confirm Alex's situation too.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Important question for Blake:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance while they are in care?**

This is relevant because if the local council is paying for Blake's accommodation or upkeep, you generally cannot receive Child Benefit for them.

Do you know if the council is contributing financially to Blake's care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming. Since the council is not paying for Blake's accommodation or maintenance, you meet this requirement for Blake.

Now let me just confirm the same check for **Alex**, who lives at home with you normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex**, who lives at home with you:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(Since Alex lives at home with you normally, I expect the answer is no – the council isn't paying – but I just need to confirm this to complete the assessment.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything for Alex. Alex just lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent, thank you for confirming. I now have all the information I need to provide you with the eligibility outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Based on all the information you've provided, I can now give you the outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for **both Alex and Blake**.

---

### **Alex (age 13)** ✅ ELIGIBLE

Alex qualifies because:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in their own name
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- Alex lives with you at home
- No one else is claiming for them
- The council is not paying for their accommodation

---

### **Blake (age 15)** ✅ ELIGIBLE

Blake qualifies because:
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- Not receiving benefits in their own name
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You live in the UK
- You contribute financially to Blake's upkeep
- No one else is claiming for them
- Although in local authority care, it's been under 8 weeks
- The council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance

---

### **Next Steps to Claim:**

1. **Apply online** or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

### **Important Note:**

⚠️ **For Blake:** Keep in mind that if Blake remains in local authority care beyond 8 weeks, you may need to reassess eligibility unless Blake starts spending at least 24 hours per week at home, or 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ days in a row at home.

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through tax. You can still claim but should be aware of this.

---

For more information, visit: [How to claim Child Benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 13) is eligible for Child Benefit because: 1) They are under 16 years old, 2) They are not working, 3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name, 4) They are not in an apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, 8) Alex lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or fostering), and 9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 15) is eligible for Child Benefit because: 1) They are under 16 years old, 2) They are not working, 3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name, 4) They are not in an apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Blake as you contribute to their upkeep financially, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, 8) Although Blake is in local authority care, they have been in care for approximately 4 weeks which is under the 8-week limit, and 9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. Alex lives at home and meets all standard eligibility criteria. Blake is in local authority care but has been there for only about a month (under the 8-week threshold), the council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance, and the claimant contributes financially to Blake's upkeep. Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in apprenticeships.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child been in care for 8 weeks or less, OR do they spend at least 24 hours a week at home?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 8 weeks in care. Exception if child spends 24+ hours/week at home. Can claim again if child spends 2 consecutive nights/week or 7+ days in a row at home.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 13) is eligible for Child Benefit because: 1) They are under 16 years old, 2) They are not working, 3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name, 4) They are not in an apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Alex as they live with you, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, 8) Alex lives at home normally (not in hospital, care, or fostering), and 9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 15) is eligible for Child Benefit because: 1) They are under 16 years old, 2) They are not working, 3) They do not receive any benefits in their own name, 4) They are not in an apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Blake as you contribute to their upkeep financially, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them, 8) Although Blake is in local authority care, they have been in care for approximately 4 weeks which is under the 8-week limit, and 9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. Alex lives at home and meets all standard eligibility criteria. Blake is in local authority care but has been there for only about a month (under the 8-week threshold), the council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance, and the claimant contributes financially to Blake's upkeep. Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in apprenticeships.
